sure that we cover as many of these as we possibly can. That 40
minutes just flew by. Didn’t it?
Let’s get into the questions, Josh. Let’s see what we’ve got here.
Josh: 	First, I just want to let you know I’m very relaxed after that one-nostril
breathing technique, as you were explaining it.
Jon: 	I was going to do it myself, but I would have sounded kind of funny on

Josh: 	This is from Amy. Can you tell us more about how to provide value
and being vague enough to create the desire for the product? Are
there other things besides the right time, that kind of thing?
Jon: 	Oh, sure. Yeah. Amy, tell me what your product is. Let’s see if we
can play with this here.
Josh: 	She has marriage as her product. Strong marriage __ (1:02) is her
products. I’m guessing it’s strengthening marriage.
Jon: 	Okay, strengthening marriage. Give me a tip that you would give
someone. If I was married, give me a tip that would help my marriage
be strengthened.
Josh: 	Spend 8 hours a week alone together.
Jon: 	Okay. I love that tip, by the way. It’s an awesome tip. You can totally
phrase that into an enjoy tip. That’s easy.

-- 1 of 17 --

2
“I’m going to give you a great way to enjoy bonding with someone.
You just set aside 8 hours. It’s kind of fun to clock the time on a
calendar, etc.”
That tip, it’s very difficult to be vague. But here’s how you can do it.
Just block out 8 hours alone. That’s all you need. That’s a little over
an hour a day alone, or however you segment it out.
Then you could say, “There’s a way that you could make that time
even more productive and even more intimate.” Like, for example, you
might say, in your product – I’m making this up, because I don’t know –
you might say that, “You know what? If you can make just one night a
3-hour date night,” or whatever, “your relationship will go to the next
level.”
So, you don’t tell them the date night secret. What you say is, “There’s
one secret that I tell people. It’s a way to make this even better. And
I’ll tell you how to get a hold of that in just a minute.”
So, that’s how you would open the loop there.
Amy, does that help?
Josh: 	She said, “Cool.”
Jon: 	Let me walk through the process, what I did with Amy, real quick. I
know nothing about her product at all.
I’ve written copy for one couples thing. It wasn’t marriage specifically,
but it was couples. So, I know a little bit about that.
But what I was doing is I was searching, in my mind. First of all, she
told me the tip. The tip was awesome, because it had a number in it,
and it was something like, “Okay, 8 hours a week.” So, 8 hours a week
to spend alone with somebody.
The very first thing I thought of is, “Hold it! I can do that! I can do that!
I’m a workaholic, but I can do that.” Let’s say I’m thinking that.
Here’s a great way to think of tips. When you write down a t8ip, think
of the objections people will make to it. Write down every objection
people will make to that tip.
Like, for example, “8 hours a week? Hell, I work 10-million hours a
week! How am I going to spend that time?” You could say, “It’s easy
to do this, if you break it into 30-minute segments.” “Oh, okay. I can
do that.”

-- 2 of 17 --

3
So, you might want to cover those objections. Like here’s a tip within a
tip, so to say.
When she gave me the tip of 8 hours, I immediately started thinking,
“Okay, would there be a way to put that tip on steroids?” In other
words, what’s something that would maximize that tip?
Well, more than likely, it’s going to have to do with something like
spending more time, like have a date night in the week. If you break
that up into 8, you’re going to spend more than 1.2 hours on a date
night, probably. So, you’ll spend like 3 or 4 hours on a date night. Or
maybe the tip has to do with finding a free babysitter.
It could be so specific. Like, “I’ll give you a tip on how to find a free
babysitter. Just hang on with me for a minute.”
Well, a lot of married couples would go, “I’d love to do that, but I don’t
have a babysitter.” “What if you could find one for free?”
Again, I’m making this stuff up, but this is how you want to think about
the process, when it comes down to thinking of your tips. Think of all
the objections that someone would have, and then solve those
objections before you give your tip. That way, inside the tip, you can
address at least most of those objections.
You can’t address all of them. There’s going to be a section that we
give you inside the video sales letter to address every objection. So,
don’t worry about that. That’s coming up in a few weeks. But you can
certainly address the biggest ones within that tip.
Was that helpful for everybody? I’ll tell you what, somebody else do
this. Let’s do this a few more times. If somebody else is having issues
with this, let’s throw out your product, let’s throw out the tip that you
want to give, and let’s see what we come up with. I think that will be
very helpful for everybody.
Josh: 	Totally. While it’s coming in, let me go ahead and get to the next
question here.
This is from Leo. Leo says, “In working with stroke survivors, they all
have the same problem but different outcomes or needs, meaning
sometimes they’ll have speech impediments, sometimes they have
spasticity.
So, how do I know which tips to focus on? If you don’t hit their tip,
obviously, they’re going to lose a lot of sales.

-- 3 of 17 --

4
Jon: 	Okay. A couple things. First of all, what’s your name? And second of
all, who’s your target audience?
I would think that the target audience would not be the patients
themselves, it would be their loved ones. Am I right?
Josh: 	He says both are his target.
Jon: 	Okay, both are the target? That’s going to be a challenge. Depending
on the severity of the stroke, the cognitive abilities of the person
watching this is going to be relatively low. Is that true? Is it true that
the stroke victims would have lower cognition? Some people are
completely impaired by it. Right?
Caller: 	Yeah. Usually, the stroke survivors themselves are looking up the
information. They’re usually ones who can talk with one hand or they
can talk, or actually understand communication. And then, it’s usually
the caregivers, if it’s the ones where they don’t understand
communication properly, or whatever.
So, my question was more if I only target speech recovery, then that
loses anyone who’s there for spasticity. How do I know which one to
really target, or how do I target both at once, because some do have
both?
Jon: 	Is there a top 5 list or a top 3 list of people that hit your site? You may
have to make more than one VSL, which it’s the same VSL just with
different tips and benefits.
But people do that all the time. Like, for example, “If you suffer from a
speech disorder, if you suffer from motor dysfunction, click here, that
can actually increase your click-throughs, by the way, to your video
sales letter, because it’s very specific to them.
Then, it’s literally just adjusting a few phrases and sentences within
your tip formula.
Caller: 	Okay. I’ll have a look at doing the individual ones, then.
Jon: 	That’s one way of doing it. The other way of doing it is to take the top
3 things that they deal with and keep mentioning that over and over
again.
So, like in the very first of your video, which we’re going to cover in the
next segment, by the way, the very first of your video, when you go into
the snap suggestion and the final connection, you’re going to be giving
them what we call a big promise. What are they going to get for
watching this video.

-- 4 of 17 --

5
And inside that big promise, you’re going to say, “I’m going to give you
solutions that you can apply today, right now, that will help anyone that
is suffering from speech impairment, motor impairment, blah, blah,
blah, blah.”
And just make sure that those blahs are covered in the tips.
So, in a tip, you might want to say, “This is an awesome tip for
someone that’s specifically dealing with a speech issue. And we’re
going to get to those other issues in just a moment.”
So, if you can just do the top issues and leave it open-ended, if you list
off the tips in your big promise that you’re going to give them, if you’re
only going to cover 3 dysfunctions, but let’s make that the top 3 that
are getting your hits, then that’s what you can do to make one video
sales letter.
To optimize this process, you would want to make video sales letters
for each.
Caller: 	Okay, thanks.
Jon: 	Make sense?

From our feedback that we got last week was that people really loved it

put you online, or whatever, because I think that helps everyone get

Josh: 	We got a lot of yeses in there. No no’s.

Josh: 	Okay. I can go straight to those, if you want me to. We have a bunch

Everyone out there has a suggestion on their product. Right? No one

So, what’s one tip that you want to give away inside your video sales

Josh: 	Brad said his tip is to follow direct response principles. He says all

Jon: 	Brad, that’s awesome. A direct response principle. That leaves it wide

So, that would be a great how-to tip. How do you measure direct

Being in a similar market, I can think of a ton of things you’d want to
avoid. Avoid advertising that has no metrics, go down the list here.
But things that you can get very useful tips for.
Is there any problems you see with your tips, like specifically?
Josh: 	Guys, as you’re putting your tips in to make sure that you give some

Jon: 	A good model for this is like the tip that was given a while ago, spend 8

If you guys can think of tips like that, these are little ah-ha moments,

for your user, that literally inspires them to stop and go, “Wow! I did
not know that!”
Josh: 	Here’s one. Brad got back to us here. He says, “Less than 5% of

Jon: 	That’s just wide open. That’s like tip-building gold. “Today, I’m going

So, that would be the tease to it, the very first in your big promise,

You can say something like, “The direct marketing 5%, blah, blah,

Josh: 	Here’s another tip from Katherine. She’s got a product on helping

That’s sort of a tease, really. But __ (15:47).

8
And then you can say, “I’ll tell you a great alternative to that later on in
the video. Just keep watching.” That alternative may be found within
your product.
Josh: 	This one is from Zack. This is for a wrestling workout program. It
says, “Avoid long-distance running, especially in-season,” is his tip.
Jon: 	Okay, avoid long-distance running. It would be something else,
wouldn’t it? This also kind of plays up my alley.
You’re wanting them to avoid excess cardiovascular activity during the
season? Is that what the thrust of the tip is? Is that a common
problem with wrestlers?
Josh: 	He rewrote it here. Let me read you the rewrite. He said, “You want to
avoid long-distance running, especially during the in-season. The
long-distance runs will actually train you into a slower, less-explosive
wrestler.
Jon: 	Okay, there you go. The one thing you absolutely have to avoid is
getting into wrestling shape and condition by doing long-distance runs,
especially during the season. And the reason why, you want to get
specific here. I was a physiology minor, so I know a little bit about that.
Then, I quit.
So, you want to get into the physiology of fast-twitch and slow-twitch
muscle fibers. Maybe you can say, “If you do this, you’re going to train
your body into this kind of state, and you don’t want to go into that kind
of state, you want to be in this explosive state.”
So, you can give them an explicit reason why.
And then, and this is the key to this, if you just end the tip there, it’s a
good tip. But it’s not a good sales tip. A good sales tip would be like,
“There are 4 or 5 other things that you really need to avoid. I’ll tell you
about where you can find out about those later.” And where they can
find out about them is where? Inside your product.
That’s how the tips work their magic for sales. How they work their
magic for affiliates is content. You’ve just given them content. Like a
lot of wrestlers I know run all the time. What was that wrestling movie
that was real famous in the 80’s? Matthew Modine was in it. Anyway,
he ran and ran and ran all the time, and half the movie was him
running. So, that’s really a bad way to make weight is what you’re
saying.
So then, you want to turn around and you want to say, “There’s a
much, much better way to make weight, that I’ll tell you about in a

-- 8 of 17 --

9
moment. And that’s either another tip, so you go into, “This is how you
can actually make this more fun, you can enjoy making weight,
because you just want to do blank.”
It’s kind of building itself here.
Josh: 	Vision Quest is the name of that movie.
Jon: 	Vision Quest. Thank you.
Josh: 	Cool. Let me go back to one of the first questions here, from Becky.
Becky’s having a hard time understanding the difference between
selling something as a product versus something that she does for
them. She does a service for them. You’d want to know how she
gives them tips. Would she explain it as if they would be doing it
themselves, or is she going to explain how the tips will be whenever
they hire her? How would she word those?
Jon: 	Service industries work the best when you talk about what to avoid.
This is what you’re trying to say. You’re trying to say, “Look, you need
to come to me because I have this service. And the #1 mistake people
like you make, because a lot of people like you are very successful or
driven,” or whatever they are, “is to not come to a person like me.”
I don’t know what your service is, but you say, “To go to blank instead
of coming here.”
Then you say, “Here’s all the nightmare stories that happen when you
got to blank.” That’s what you want to avoid.
Again, the enjoyment factor can just kind of be like, “Look, wouldn’t you
rather have total peace of mind, freeing up your time and your energy
to concentrate on your business? Well, that’s the enjoyable factor.
That way, you can have more fun in your business. You can enjoy it
more.”
So, that’s really the entire enjoyment. It’s not even a tip section. Like I
said, it’s a future-pacing section. You can do one or the other. And
you go, “Let me tell you how to do that, right now. How to do that is,”
and then the how-to is basically hire me.
Then, you want to build up a how-to for specifics. What do you do
that’s so specific? There’s something that you do that’s specific and
unique. What is it? And that is how you build the how-to section.
Remember, that’s built off your primary benefit, but it’s built off the
driving force behind the primary benefit.

-- 9 of 17 --

10
So, let me give you the example. My primary benefit is a product-
based primary benefit. In other words, the primary benefit is you get to
eat your favorite foods. The driving force is called “the Caloric
Staggering Principle.”
So, what is your primary benefit? And what is the force behind it? If
your primary benefit is blank, what do you give your clients, why does
that work?
That way, you can build a case by saying, “What you do instead is you
want to go to a blank. Take advantage of the services of a blank.”
The services of a blank can give you this, this and the other, and
here’s how you do it. And, by the way, that’s exactly what I do. That’s
just how you would lead into it.
Josh: 	Okay, so Becky’s service, by the way, is she writes letters for people,
so they can avoid foreclosure.
Jon: 	Now that you tell me that, that makes it so easy. Foreclosure, that’s
ripe with fear. There’s fear all over this. So, you can walk right into
this.
So, your how-to tip can literally be, “Here’s exactly how you can avoid
foreclosure.” You are selling a service, but you’re really selling them a
product that’s a service. You know what I’m saying? In other words,
your how-to, you can open, right out of the gates, “I’m going to tell you,
today, 3 secrets to avoiding foreclosure. One of them costs less than a
penny to produce,” which I’m thinking of the piece of paper that you
would write it on. So, you can think of something intriguing like that.
“Less than a penny to produce.” That means produce, physically, not
necessarily do. Those are the kind of tricks you want to think of when
you approach this.
The how-to behind this is really strong. “Here’s the 1¢ trick that I told
you about. You have to write a letter. You just have to know what kind
of letters to write.” And I would say, “Here are the 3 types of letters
that you can write. You can write this kind of letter, this kind of letter,
this kind of letter. Or, you can let me do it for you.”
This goes into what we’re going to cover in later modules, the done-for-
you ideas.
So, the done-for-you ideas is huge. It works fantastic in video sales
letters.

-- 10 of 17 --

11
So, you can say, “You can labor over writing this letter and guess what
the right words are, and hope that it gets put right, or you can use my
formula that I’ve created, and I will do this for you.”
At this point, you’ve gone over the how-to. The how-to is you want to
write a letter, and the product is I’ll do it for you. Does that help?
Josh: 	Yes. She said, “That helps.”
This is a good question here. “How many times do we do the 6 steps
of the tip formula with the VSL?” And the second part of this question
is, “Do we do the 3-tip loop for each tip, as well?
Jon: 	No. The 3-tip loop is the 3 tips that you use. Again, it’s open the how-
to loop, then you go into the avoid, then you go to the enjoy, then you
go into the how-to close.
That’s the loop. Use it one time. Those are 3 different tips. And
you’re only going to use 3 categories of tips inside your VSL. You’re
going to use the enjoy, avoid, how-to.
I think that may answer your question. If it doesn’t, let me know. But
inside each of those tips is the 6 patterns that you follow, which starts
off by what’s the benefit and why do it, and what is it. That’s the
structure you want to follow, just to create each tip.
So, just think of each tip as its own little animal. That’s why we broke
out the slides this way. If you think, “I’m going to do just my avoid tips
right now, I’m going to make a list over here, I’m going to make a list of
the 10 things I think someone should avoid.” I’m going to narrow that
down to 3.
I’m going to say, “Here are 3 things you absolutely need to avoid. You
need to avoid this, you need to avoid this, and you need to avoid this.”
You don’t have to do that loop on all 3 of those things, if that’s what
you’re asking.
Josh: 	Yeah, that was the question.
Jon: 	You want to find the commonality inside each of those things. You
can. You can do that loop on all 3 of those things. If you do that, it’s
just going to make a longer section, and it’s okay. Again, I’ve seen
video sales letters 45 minutes long, that convert great. It depends on
the length of your video sales letter, how long you feel you want to go.
But you can. Like I said, you can loop the different ones, or where’s
the common denominator.

-- 11 of 17 --

12
Like, for example, I’m just thinking off the top of my head here, if I say
“You want to avoid these 3 foods if you absolutely want to lose weight.
You want to avoid white flour, refined sugar, and juice.”
Then, all I have to say is, “All 3 of these foods have one thing in
common. And there are a lot more foods, by the way, that I’m not
telling you about. I’ll tell you where you can find out later, but you’re
probably eating them.”
See, I kind of do this naturally now. Sorry. I go into VSL mode all the
time.
Anyway, “All 3 of these foods share one thing in common: they’re all
refined sugars or they’re all refined starches. Everything is refined
carbohydrate.”
So, I’ll find the common factor there. So, if you take your multiple tips
and you find the common factor, you can just do one loop.
Josh: 	I wanted to go ahead and say Jonas’ tip here, because I thought it was

Jon: 	That’s great. I know all about oxytocin. It’s kind of cool, when you’re

would say, “This mysterious little hormone that’s responsible for all the
pleasures.” I would really hype that thing up a little bit.
I love how you ended it up, “I’ll tell you how to do this in a moment.”
That’s your how-to tease, for sure.
But, it’s also what you want to avoid. This is ripe for avoidance. You
want to say, “The #1 mistake that guys make when they’re trying to

them, is to try to be erotic.”
So, you go into the details of why they want to avoid that. You want to

save the oxytocin thing as your primary how-to. “But there are other

Josh: 	Yeah. He led into it by saying, “The #1 thing to say to a collection

Jon: 	That’s an awesome tip. If I was going to stop credit hassling and I saw

Again, we always want to think about opening loops. We give you an
example of how to open a how-to loop and how to close it. But
whenever you say anything in your video sales letter where you know
that the whole answer is in your product, you can say, “I’ll tell you more
about that. Just keep watching.” It’s something that you can repeat
over and over again.

Did that make sense? That’s an awesome tip. I say keep it, by all
means. I’m sure there’s more to it than that. In fact, you may want to
even say what it would be.
The very first thing I thought of, when I said that, is, “I don’t know. I
don’t get calls from creditors.” But I did back when I was in my 20’s. I
don’t get those anymore. I did back then, and at least for a brief period
in life.
My first thought, going back in those days, was, “Um, if I said that, they
would just ignore me. They would just keep calling me.” Like Tracy
would call me day one, and Kevin would call me day 2, so, I’d have to
keep telling this to Kevin and Tracy, and everybody else who calls.
That might be something you say. “Here’s what you tell them, blank.
And you say, “Now, you may be thinking: are they going to ignore me?
What if somebody else calls? I’ll deal with that in just a moment. I’ll
tell you how to deal with that later on in the video.” And you do.
You say, “Remember when I talked about how to deal with all of those
calls and all of those creditors? There’s a lot more to it than this, and
that’s why I created this product, blank. It covers blank, blank, blank,
blank.”
Does that make sense? Does that resonate with what you’re actually
talking about?
Before we go over one more, let’s ask the ubiquitous question that we
always ask, and that is, is everybody getting a lot out of this? Is the

I know you’re going to have to look over the notes again, and you’re

are you learning something?
Other than that, is anyone not learning anything? Is anyone going,

Jon: 	Really? Wow. I’ll tell you what, I can sell the crap out of your service.

You’re talking to guys like me, basically, or a lot of guys that have

I understand the brick-and-mortar business, even though I’m in the info
business, because I had a brick-and-mortar business.
The application of this is very simple. If I saw any sort of video sales
letter and said, “Look, here’s how you can take your brick-and-mortar
business and get stellar reviews automatically, using a Smartphone,
and boost your online reputation through the roof.” That would be
lead-in.
The video sales letter, we can’t go over the whole thing because you’re
going to have to go back over and go through the first 2 sessions here,
but just the tips part is really easy.
“Here’s the one thing you definitely want to avoid if you’re trying to get
market presence on Google: you want to avoid spending money on
traffic. You want to avoid spending money on banner ads, and avoid
spending money on SEO, avoid spending money on” – I can go down
the list – “Trust Guard, Better Business Bureau.
This is easy. I know, because the Better Business Bureau sucks. I will
preach that. Everyone online, Google “Better Business Bureau fraud,”
and you’ll find exactly how fraudulent that company is.
I can just go down the line of horror stories here that make it very easy
on what to tell business owners to avoid.
You can build an entire sales letter around, “Look, I’m going to tell you
the 3 biggest things to avoid if you want more exposure for your
business. And I’m going to give you a solution, at the end of this video,
to give you automatic exposure using nothing but a Smartphone;
exposure that you can control, reviews that you can“ – I don’t know if
you can control the reviews or not – etc.”
Then, you can go into, “Here’s what most brick-and-mortar businesses
do. They try to advertise in newspapers, they try to get in the Better
Business Bureau. I can just go down the line, man.
Honestly, I almost want to write it now. I don’t know how on earth this
could not be a more apt business for this.

17
But then again, I think that’s true for just about anybody. There’s video
sales letters from restaurants, for crying out loud.
With that said, hopefully that inspired some thought there, because I
would definitely be thinking, “Look, here’s what most business owners
that are brick-and-mortar are doing to get exposure, and it’s all
completely a waste of money and time, or at least most of it is. And
then, it’s trial and error if it’s not, and you’re going to waste a lot of
money. Why not just pay this small amount and get this done on an
automated basis? The higher up you are in Yelp and the higher up
you are in Google reviews, the higher you up you are in search
engines.”
I can just go on and on here. Did that help at all?
Josh: 	He said it helped. Great.
Jon: 	Cool. Seriously, stick around for this thing, because I think your
business is completely suited to this, mainly because it’s a digital

concept, too.
By the way, please don’t take this the wrong way. If anyone else has
any questions like that, like, “I’m wondering if my business is suitable

was with a consulting business. Just because of what they were

one of them, for sure.
Again, most people who are on this course understand that there is a
